section.title: Not to Us, but to Your Name
section.sectionRationale: This third Hallel psalm refuses every claim to human glory, redirecting all praise to God alone while contrasting the living, active LORD with the mute, powerless idols of the nations, then closing with a call for the living to bless the LORD forever.
section.thesis: All glory belongs to God alone, never to His people, because He is a living God who acts as He pleases, unlike the lifeless idols that can neither speak, see, nor save, and so trusting Him alone is the only fitting response of the living.
section.frameNote: Five movements: glory refused for self and given to God alone, verses 1-3; idols exposed as powerless and lifeless, verses 4-8; a threefold call to trust the LORD as help and shield, verses 9-11; the promise of His blessing on small and great alike, verses 12-15; and the closing call for the living to praise Him forever, verses 16-18.

units.label: Not to us, but to Your name
units.note: glory refused for self, given to God alone

verses.text: Not to us, O LORD, not to us, but to Your name be the glory, because of Your loving devotion, because of Your faithfulness.
Why should the nations say, "Where is their God?"
Our God is in heaven; He does as He pleases.

units.label: Their idols are silver and gold
units.note: mute, powerless, made by human hands

verses.text: Their {{elilim|idols}} are silver and gold, made by the hands of men.
They have mouths, but cannot speak; they have eyes, but cannot see;
they have ears, but cannot hear; they have noses, but cannot smell;
they have hands, but cannot feel; they have feet, but cannot walk; they cannot even clear their throats.
Those who make them become like them, as do all who trust in them.

units.label: O Israel, trust in the LORD

verses.text: O Israel, trust in the LORD! He is their help and shield.
O house of Aaron, trust in the LORD! He is their help and shield.
You who fear the LORD, trust in the LORD! He is their help and shield.
The LORD is mindful of us; He will bless us. He will bless the house of Israel; He will bless the house of Aaron;
He will bless those who fear the LORD, small and great alike.
May the LORD give you increase, both you and your children.
May you be blessed by the LORD, the Maker of heaven and earth.

units.label: It is we who will bless the LORD

verses.text: The highest heavens belong to the LORD, but the earth He has given to mankind.
It is not the dead who praise the LORD, nor any who descend into silence.
But it is we who will bless the LORD, both now and forevermore. Hallelujah!

elilim.gloss: Idols, worthless things, a term related to a root meaning nothingness. The word itself mocks the objects it names, calling attention to their impotence in stark contrast to the living, acting God of verse 3.

basic.context: This psalm refuses to let Israel's people receive credit for God's own faithfulness, redirecting all glory to His name, then satirizes the lifeless idols of the surrounding nations before calling every group in Israel, priests and people alike, to trust the LORD who alone is a living help and shield.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm's structure, from refusing self-glory to exposing idols to calling for trust to promising blessing to demanding living praise, models a worship that is entirely God-centered, rooted in His character rather than in human achievement or manufactured religion.
basic.originalLanguages: The idols are called elilim, worthless things, a term whose very sound in Hebrew mocks their pretension to power, verse 4. Unlike them, the LORD does as He pleases, verse 3, acting freely and sovereignly rather than remaining inert like silver and gold shaped by human hands.
basic.typology: The psalm's insistence that only the living can praise the LORD, verse 17, anticipates the fuller resurrection hope of the New Testament, and its warning that idolaters become like their idols, verse 8, anticipates Paul's teaching that people are transformed into the image of whatever they worship, whether idols or the living Christ, 2 Corinthians 3:18.
basic.christ: This psalm's refusal to glorify anyone but God, not to us, but to Your name be the glory, verse 1, anticipates Christ's own perfect self-emptying obedience that results in the Father highly exalting Him, Philippians 2:9-11, so that every knee bows to the name above every name. The psalm's contrast between lifeless idols and the living God who does as He pleases is fulfilled in Christ, the image of the invisible God, Colossians 1:15, who alone gives true sight, hearing, and life to those who trust Him.
basic.god: God alone deserves glory, acting in sovereign freedom, unlike the mute and powerless idols fashioned by human hands. He is mindful of His people, promising blessing to small and great alike, and He is the Maker of heaven and earth, worthy of the praise only the living can offer Him.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 44:9-20 similarly exposes the folly of crafting and trusting idols made by human hands. Acts 17:29 echoes this psalm's logic in Paul's sermon to the Athenians. Philippians 2:9-11 shows the pattern of glory refused for self and given wholly to God fulfilled in Christ's exaltation.
basic.oneStory: All glory belongs to the living God alone, never to lifeless idols or to human achievement, a pattern of self-emptying glory-giving fulfilled perfectly in Christ, who refused to grasp at glory for Himself yet was exalted by the Father to the name above every name, so that the truly living, in Him, forever bless the LORD.

context.literary: A five-part hymn moving from doxology, verses 1-3, to satire against idols, verses 4-8, to a threefold liturgical call to trust, verses 9-11, to a blessing formula, verses 12-15, and a closing contrast between the silent dead and the praising living, verses 16-18, with the repeated refrain He is their help and shield binding the central section together.
context.historical: Likely composed for a setting where Israel faced the mockery of surrounding nations, perhaps a post-exilic community rebuilding its identity amid pagan neighbors who questioned where Israel's God was, sharpening the psalm's insistence on trusting the LORD rather than any visible, manufactured deity.
context.canonical: As the third Hallel psalm, Psalms 113-118, sung at Passover, it stands between Psalm 114's celebration of the exodus and Psalm 116's personal thanksgiving, anchoring the collection's confidence in the LORD's living power against the backdrop of the nations' dead gods.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To strip away every ground for human boasting, directing all praise to God's own character, His loving devotion and faithfulness, while contrasting His living power with the powerlessness of idols, and to call every level of Israel's community to trust and bless Him as the only fitting response.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific ancient Near Eastern context of national mockery and idol worship; it prescribes the perpetual pattern of refusing self-glory, giving all credit to God, and offering Him living praise and trust rather than misplaced confidence in anything humanly made.

debates.question: Does verse 17's statement that the dead do not praise the LORD deny any conscious existence or worship after death?

views.label: The verse reflects the Old Testament's typically shadowy view of Sheol without addressing later, fuller revelation of resurrection hope
views.case: Old Testament writers frequently describe death and Sheol as a realm of silence, Psalm 6:5 and Psalm 30:9, using this motif to urge living worshipers to praise now, without intending a comprehensive doctrine of the afterlife that later revelation would expand.

views.label: The verse specifically emphasizes urgency for present praise rather than making a comprehensive doctrinal claim about all conscious existence after death
views.case: The psalm's rhetorical purpose is pastoral urgency, motivating the living to praise now, similar to Hezekiah's psalm in Isaiah 38:18-19, and later revelation, such as Revelation 14:13, describes the dead in Christ continuing to bless Him, showing the Old Testament's emphasis was on present urgency, not final anthropology.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the verse's chief function is to press urgency: praise God now, while you live, rather than presuming an indefinite opportunity, a call that gains even greater force in light of the fuller resurrection hope Christ secures.

study.originalLanguages: Israel's idols are elilim, worthless things, a term that in Hebrew sound itself mocks their pretension, verse 4, in sharp contrast to the LORD who does as He pleases, verse 3, acting with living, sovereign freedom rather than remaining inert like silver and gold shaped by human hands.

typology.type: Refusing glory for self, giving it wholly to God, verse 1
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ's self-emptying obedience, resulting in the Father exalting Him to the name above every name, Philippians 2:9-11.

typology.type: Becoming like what one worships, verse 8
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled inversely in believers who, beholding the glory of the Lord, are transformed into His image from one degree of glory to another, 2 Corinthians 3:18.

study.christ: This psalm's insistence that glory belongs not to us but to God's name alone, verse 1, anticipates Christ's own pattern of self-emptying obedience, for which God highly exalted Him and bestowed on Him the name above every name, Philippians 2:9-11. The contrast between lifeless idols, unable to speak, see, or act, and the living God who does as He pleases, verse 3, is answered in Christ, the image of the invisible God, Colossians 1:15, who alone gives sight to the blind and speech to the mute, proving Himself the living God this psalm proclaims.
study.god: God alone deserves all glory, acting in sovereign freedom, unlike lifeless idols shaped by human hands. He is mindful of His people at every level, small and great, promising blessing and increase, and as the Maker of heaven and earth, He alone is worthy of the praise only the truly living can offer.

crossReferences.note: Exposes the folly of crafting and worshiping idols made by human hands, paralleling this psalm's satire in verses 4-8.
Paul echoes this psalm's logic before the Athenians, arguing against divine images made by human art and imagination.
Shows the pattern of refusing glory for self and giving it to God fulfilled perfectly in Christ's exaltation by the Father.

study.oneStory: All glory belongs to the living God alone, never to lifeless idols or human achievement, a pattern of glory refused for self and given wholly to God that is fulfilled perfectly in Christ, whose self-emptying obedience led to His exaltation by the Father, so that the truly living, united to Him, forever bless the LORD both now and forevermore.

quiz.q: To whom does the psalmist insist all glory belongs in verse 1?

options.text: To the LORD's name alone, because of His loving devotion and faithfulness.
options.note: The repeated 'not to us' emphatically refuses any human claim to the glory that belongs to God alone.

options.text: To Israel's own military achievements.
options.note: The verse explicitly says 'not to us,' rejecting any claim to glory based on human achievement.

options.text: Equally to Israel and to the LORD.
options.note: The psalm gives no share of glory to Israel; it belongs entirely to God's name.

options.text: To the craftsmen who build the temple.
options.note: The glory is directed to God's name and character, not to any human builders or craftsmen.

quiz.q: According to verses 5-7, what can the idols not do despite having mouths, eyes, ears, and hands?

options.text: Speak, see, hear, smell, feel, or walk.
options.note: This catalogue of inability exposes the total powerlessness of manufactured idols.

options.text: They can do all these things but choose not to.
options.note: The text says they cannot, not that they choose not to; they are simply incapable.

options.text: They can speak but cannot see.
options.note: The text denies all these senses and abilities to the idols, not merely one.

options.text: They can walk but cannot speak.
options.note: The psalm denies walking as well as speaking to these lifeless idols.

quiz.q: What warning does verse 8 give about those who trust in idols?

options.text: They become like them, lifeless and powerless.
options.note: This warns that worship shapes the worshiper into the likeness of the object worshiped.

options.text: They gain the idol's silver and gold as a reward.
options.note: The verse warns of becoming like the idols, not of financial gain.

options.text: They are immediately punished with exile.
options.note: The specific warning given is becoming like the idols, not a statement about exile.

options.text: They become wiser through the experience.
options.note: The warning is the opposite; trusting in idols leads to becoming as lifeless as they are, not gaining wisdom.

quiz.q: Who is called to trust in the LORD in the threefold refrain of verses 9-11?

options.text: Israel, the house of Aaron, and all who fear the LORD.
options.note: This widening circle includes the nation, its priesthood, and ultimately every reverent worshiper.

options.text: Only the king and his royal court.
options.note: The call extends to Israel, the priesthood, and all who fear the LORD, not merely royalty.

options.text: Only foreign nations, not Israel itself.
options.note: The call is addressed specifically to Israel and the house of Aaron, God's own covenant people, not foreign nations.

options.text: Only the wealthy among God's people.
options.note: The call extends to small and great alike, verse 13, not merely the wealthy.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's pattern of refusing self-glory anticipate Christ?

options.text: Christ's own self-emptying obedience led the Father to exalt Him to the name above every name.
options.note: Philippians 2:9-11 shows this exact pattern, glory refused for self and given wholly to God, fulfilled in Christ's exaltation.

options.text: It has no meaningful connection to Christ or the New Testament.
options.note: Philippians 2:9-11 directly parallels this psalm's pattern of humility resulting in exaltation by God.

options.text: It predicts the exact date of Christ's return.
options.note: The psalm's concern is glory rightly directed to God, not a chronological prediction.

options.text: It shows Christ competing with the Father for glory.
options.note: The pattern is the opposite; Christ's humility results in the Father's exaltation of Him, not competition for glory.

questions: The psalm insists that glory belongs not to us but to God's name alone. Where are you tempted to take credit for something that was really God's faithfulness and loving devotion at work?

The idols described have mouths, eyes, and hands but cannot use any of them. What modern 'idols,' things that promise help but cannot truly hear, see, or save, are you tempted to trust instead of God?
Verse 8 warns that we become like what we worship. Looking honestly at your habits and affections this week, what does your life suggest you are actually worshiping?

The threefold call to trust extends from Israel to the priesthood to everyone who fears the LORD, small and great alike. Where do you need to hear this call to trust personally, not just as a general truth about God?
Christ's self-emptying humility led to His exaltation by the Father. Where is God calling you to release your grip on receiving credit or glory, trusting Him to lift you in His own time and way?

translationNotes.note: Idols renders elilim, a Hebrew term whose sound and root evoke worthlessness or nothingness, a built-in mockery of the objects it names.

A variant Hebrew manuscript tradition, along with the Septuagint and Syriac, reads 'O house of Israel' rather than simply 'O Israel,' a minor textual difference that does not affect the psalm's meaning.

The dead do not praise the LORD reflects the Old Testament's typically shadowy portrayal of Sheol, used here to press the urgency of praising God now, while alive, rather than presuming on an unlimited future opportunity.
